lines.text: "You are, in fact, standing on a tongue." That's the line that's been rattling around in my head since I finished this chapter. This whole planet is basically one giant organism, and nobody thought to ask if it wanted guests.

And the way Nyland drops that on us! After this incredibly tense heist sequence, after the Brisinger crashes, we get this archival transcript that recontextualizes everything. The committee is debating settlement locations like they're choosing a campsite, and meanwhile there's this signal from the fungal network that they just... ignore?

Anomaly 134. Conspicuously absent from their strategic calculus, as our narrator puts it. Which raises the question: who is this narrator? Because they're clearly speaking from a position of hindsight, and they're dripping with cynicism about the whole colonial project.

I love that narrative voice though. "Let's jump to the end. Which is just another beginning. This might make you a little spin sick." It's almost playful, but there's this undercurrent of tragedy. They know how this story ends, and they're warning us it's going to hurt.

Let's talk about Oliver, because I think he's more complicated than he first appears. His father designed the Compassionate Oversight Grid as a health monitoring system, something genuinely meant to help people. And now it's become, quote, "a system for total population control" under Corbin's edict. Oliver's entire mission in this chapter is stealing the tool his father built to fight that corruption.

The COG Egg! Which can make someone untraceable. It's poetic, right? The son using the father's invention to undermine what the father's other invention became. And that moment when he's at the workstation and can smell his father's cologne? I got emotional.

See, I read that scene differently. I think Oliver might be romanticizing his father. We don't know yet whether his dad was complicit in what the Grid became, or whether he died resisting it. Oliver's memories are warm, but memories lie.
That's fair, but consider this: the Egg was hidden. Deliberately concealed in a decommissioned depot. Someone wanted it preserved but not found. That suggests the father knew what was coming.

We don't have confirmation yet. But let's move to the crash, because that sequence is genuinely cinematic. The Brisinger arrives after presumably years of travel through the Fold, and within minutes it's being fired upon and torn apart. Why?

That's what I keep coming back to! The turrets fire on their own ship. Their future, as Oliver calls it. Something went very wrong, either on the ship or with whoever controls those turrets. Corbin, maybe?

And then we meet Læsa, and honestly, I don't know what to make of her. She's finger-painting in toxic sludge, she's got wounds she can't feel, she calls Oliver "Dusty" and asks what game they're playing. Is she in shock? Is she something else entirely?
Her hair is "sugarcane-bluish," her eyes match. And the way she says, "it was so quiet in the space between." She experienced something during that journey that fundamentally altered her perception.
What caught me was "that's the label on this cup" when she gives her name. Like she's reading it off herself. Like she's not entirely sure she's Læsa.

The CIX that Oliver finds is also fascinating. An AI that responded to his voice even though it was stuck in a diagnostic loop. And that runtime, thirty-three clicks, Oliver freezes when he hears it. That number means something to him.

Its been running since the Fold collapsed. The CIX units are described as "lobotomized by regulation," which is such a loaded phrase. Smart but contained, wiped every cycle to prevent "syntactic decay." Going crazy. Hallucinating essentially. There's maybe a parallel there with the human population under the Grid.
Everyone's contained. Everyone's monitored. And the only freedom is becoming a ghost in the system or... whatever Læsa is.
So here's my question going forward. What happened in between? What happened these last thirty-three clicks?
And what will Mairee do now the Brisinger just fell on her tongue? Something just fed it a very large meal.
